Item 1. Business.
The Procter & Gamble Company (the Company) is a world-leading multinational consumer goods company focused on providing trusted, branded products of superior quality, performance and value to improve the lives of consumers around the world - now and for generations to come. Our products are sold in about 180 countries and territories throughout the world. The Company was incorporated in Ohio in 1905, having first been established as a New Jersey corporation in 1890, and 
…[truncated 5656 chars]…
 do business. In the United States, many of our products and manufacturing operations are subject to one or more federal or state regulatory agencies, including the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC). We are also subject to anti-corruption laws and regulations, such as the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, and antitrust and competition laws and regulations that govern our dealings with suppliers, customers, competitors and government officials.
In addition, many foreign jurisdictions in which we do business have regulations and regulatory bodies that govern similar aspects of our operations and products, in some cases to an even more significant degree. We are also subject to expanding laws and regulations related to environmental protection and other sustainability-related matters, non-financial reporting and diligence, labor and employment, trade, taxation and privacy and data protection, including the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation and similar regulations in states within the United States and in countries around the world.
The Company has in place compliance programs and internal and external experts to help guide our business in complying with these and other existing laws and regulations that apply to us around the globe; and we have made, and plan to continue making, necessary expenditures for compliance with these laws and regulations. We also expect that our many suppliers, consultants and other third parties working on our behalf share our commitment to compliance, and we have policies and procedures in place to manage these relationships, though they inherently involve a lesser degree of control over operations and governance. We do not expect that the Company's expenditures for compliance with current government regulations, including current environmental regulations, will have a material effect on our total capital expenditures, earnings or competitive position in fiscal year 2026 as compared to prior periods.
Human Capital
. Our employees are a key source of competitive advantage. Their actions, guided by our Purpose, Values and Principles (PVPs), are critical to the long-term success of our business. We aim to retain our talented employees by offering competitive compensation and benefits, strong career development and a respectful and inclusive culture that provides equal opportunity for all.
Our Board of Directors, through the Compensation and Leadership Development Committee (C&LD Committee), provides oversight of the Company's policies and strategy relating to talent, including equality and inclusion, as well as the Company's compensation principles and practices. The C&LD Committee also evaluates and approves the Company's compensation plans, policies and programs applicable to our senior executives.
Employees
As of June 30, 2025, the Company had approximately 109,000 employees, an increase of 2% versus the prior year due to business growth. The total number of employees is an estimate of total Company employees excluding interns, co-ops, contractors and employees of joint ventures. 49% of our employees are in manufacturing roles and 28% of our employees are located in the United States. 42% of our global employees are women and 32% of our U.S. employees identify as multicultural.
Training and Development
We focus on attracting, developing and retaining the broadest pool of talent available, both from universities and the broader market. We recruit from universities across markets in which we compete and are generally able to select from the top talent. We focus on developing our employees by providing a variety of job experiences, training programs and skill development opportunities. Given our develop-from-within model for staffing most of our senior leadership positions, it is particularly important for us to ensure holistic growth and full engagement of our employees.
Equality and Inclusion
As a global consumer products company, P&G serves consumers around the world with operations in approximately 70 countries. We believe that it is good for business that our workforce is drawn from the best available talent from communities worldwide, with insights about, connectivity to and understanding of all our consumers. Further, a foundational component of P&G's strategy and success has been to foster an inclusive work environment, in which we develop and advance the very best capabilities that all our people have to offer. Globally, we aspire to reflect the diversity of the consumers we serve in the communities where we operate and are committed to creating a superior employee experience for all. Our aspiration is founded on our longstanding values and principles for equal opportunity and compliance with the law.
Compensation and Benefits
Market-competitive compensation and reward programs are critical elements of our employee value equation to attract and retain the best talent. Our total rewards programs are based on the principles of paying for performance, paying competitively versus peer companies that we compete with for talent in the marketplace and focusing on long-term success through a combination of short-term and long-term incentive programs. We also offer competitive benefit programs, including retirement plans and health insurance, in line with local country practices, with flexibility to accommodate the needs of a diverse workforce.
Sustainability
. Environmental sustainability is integrated into our business strategy. We are focused on designing and manufacturing irresistibly superior products that are more sustainable. We aim to reduce our own environmental footprint and
The Procter & Gamble Company        3
enable our consumers to reduce their footprint without compromising on the performance of the products they use. We develop and license technologies that can be used across industries to improve environmental sustainability at a broader scale.
Combined, this approach intends to positively impact the total environmental impact of the Company while driving market growth and value creation.
In 2021, the Company announced a 2040 net zero ambition. Our Climate Transition Action Plan outlines the Company's ongoing efforts toward reducing greenhouse gas emissions across scopes 1 and 2 and elements of scope 3. The Company has also declared ambitions towards purchasing renewable electricity for our operations, reducing intensity of virgin petroleum-based plastic in packaging, designing more consumer packaging to be recyclable or reusable, responsible sourcing of key forest-based commodities, improving efficiency of water usage in our operations and driving a global portfolio of water restoration projects in key water basins. While we have met or exceeded some of the goals and are making significant progress against others, scalable infrastructure and cost-effective solutions are not yet available to us to fully deliver against some of our stated ambitions.
Sustainability related disclosures included in this Annual Report, our Proxy Statement and our sustainability reports are informed by standards and guidelines such as the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD). The "materiality" thresholds in those standards and guidelines may differ from the concept of "materiality" for purposes of the federal securities laws and disclosures required by the Commission's rules in this Annual Report. References to our sustainability reports and website are for informational purposes only and neither the sustainability reports nor the other information on our website is incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K. Additional detailed information on our sustainability efforts can be found on our website at https://pginvestor.com/esg.
PG.quantitative_report:
# Procter & Gamble (PG) — Quantitative Report
**Decision date: 2026-01-01 | Evidence: point-in-time through 2025-12-31**

---

## 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue:** TTM revenue is **$85.78B**. Latest quarter (2025-09-30) delivered **$22.39B**, up **+3.0% YoY** (vs. $21.74B) and **+13.2% QoQ** (vs. $19.78B). Over the seven-quarter sample, revenue ranged from $19.78B to $22.39B, indicating modest organic growth on a volatile quarterly cadence.

**Earnings:** TTM net income is **$17.22B**. Latest-quarter EPS of **$1.95** rose **+21.1% YoY** (vs. $1.61) and **+26.6% QoQ** (vs. $1.54). TTM operating income is **$21.95B**.

**Margins (latest quarter, 2025-09-30):**
| Metric | Value | YoY Change |
|---|---|---|
| Gross margin | 51.4% (11.50B / 22.39B) | Roughly stable (52.1% prior Q1) |
| Operating margin | 26.2% (5.86B / 22.39B) | Slightly below 26.7% prior Q1 |
| Net margin | 21.3% (4.78B / 22.39B) | Up from 18.4% prior Q1 |

Supplied trailing ratios confirm: gross margin **51.3%**, operating margin **23.8%**, net margin **18.6%** (TTM-blended). Net margin expansion has outpaced operating margin, suggesting below-the-line tailwinds (tax, interest, or non-operating items).

**Balance sheet (2025-09-30):** Total assets **$127.60B**, total liabilities **$74.05B**, total equity **$53.55B**. Equity has grown from **$48.83B** (2023-12-31) to **$53.55B**, a **+9.7%** increase over nine quarters. The liability-to-equity ratio stands at **1.38x** (74.05 / 53.55). Equity ratio: **42.0%** (53.55 / 127.60).

---

## 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality — mixed signal:** EPS grew **+21.1% YoY** on only **+3.0%** revenue growth. This 7:1 leverage implies significant margin expansion, but operating margin actually *contracted* slightly (26.7% → 26.2%). The entire earnings beat appears to come from below the operating line — likely favorable tax, interest, or non-operating items — which is inherently less repeatable than core operating leverage. **Free cash flow data is entirely unavailable** across all reported periods, preventing any accrual-versus-cash comparison. This is a material blind spot.

**Balance-sheet resilience — adequate:** The equity base is expanding steadily and the capital structure is consistent across periods. No sudden jumps in liabilities. However, without debt-maturity schedules or interest-coverage ratios (not supplied), a full stress assessment is not possible. The **1.38x liability-to-equity** ratio is moderate for a consumer-staples firm.

**ROE** is **29.7%** (supplied), indicating efficient capital deployment, though this figure should be cross-checked against leverage contribution once debt breakdowns are available.

---

## 3. Valuation vs. Own History

| Metric | Latest (2025-12-31) | 2025 Range | Position in Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E (diluted) | **22.53** | 21.7 – 28.4 | **Near floor** (5th percentile) |
| P/B | **6.682** | 6.4 – 8.4 | **Near floor** (14th percentile) |
| P/S | **4.183** | n/a | — |
| Earnings yield | **4.4%** | n/a | — |

The P/E has compressed **-20.7%** over the year (28.41 → 22.53) and the P/B has compressed **-14.9%** (7.85 → 6.68). This is the steepest de-rating visible in the 12-point valuation history. The current P/E of **22.53** sits only **3.8%** above the year's trough of **21.7**.

**Context:** Despite the de-rating, fundamentals improved — YoY EPS growth of **+21.1%** and net margin expansion from **17.2%** to **18.6%** (supplied January-to-December figures). The price decline has therefore *more than offset* fundamental improvement, creating a valuation-vs-fundamentals disconnect.

---

## 4. Price Level & Return Context

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Latest close (2025-12-31) | **$143.31** |
| 52-week high | **$179.99** |
| 52-week low | **$138.14** |
| Distance from high | **-20.4%** |
| Distance from low | **+3.8%** |
| Annual return (≈252d) | **-15.5%** |
| Latest quarterly return (≈63d) | **-6.4%** |

The price has declined for **four consecutive quarters**: +0.6%, −5.5%, −5.0%, −6.4%. The cumulative drawdown from the Q1 2025 level ($169.53) to the latest is **−15.5%**, while TTM EPS *grew*. The stock is trading within **$5.17** of its 52-week low.

**Fundamental-path mismatch:** Over the annual window, the P/E contracted ~21% while implied TTM EPS grew roughly mid-single-digits. The market has aggressively re-rated the multiple downward despite expanding margins and earnings. Price momentum is uniformly negative and accelerating (quarterly losses widening from −5.0% to −6.4%).

---

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

### Upside
1. **Multiple reversion:** A move from P/E **22.53** to the mid-year average of ~**25.5** (while holding TTM EPS of ~$6.36) implies a price of ~**$162**, representing **+13%** upside.
2. **EPS growth acceleration:** If the **+21.1%** YoY growth rate persists for even two more quarters at half that pace, annual EPS could reach **$6.70–$6.85**, adding **5–8%** of earnings-driven upside independent of re-rating.
3. **Valuation floor proximity:** At **3.8%** above the year's P/E trough, mean-reversion risk is asymmetrically skewed upward in multiple terms.

### Downside
1. **Missing cash-flow data:** Cannot verify that **$17.22B** TTM net income is backed by operating cash flow. If accruals are elevated, earnings quality is lower than reported.
2. **Below-the-line earnings drivers:** The **+21.1%** EPS growth on **+3.0%** revenue growth is driven by non-operating items, which may not recur, risking an earnings miss and further de-rating.
3. **Persistent negative price momentum:** Four consecutive quarterly declines and proximity to the 52-week low (**+3.8%** cushion) suggest risk of a breakdown below **$138.14**.
4. **Revenue ceiling:** **+3.0%** YoY revenue growth in a staples business offers limited top-line leverage to justify re-rating; the P/E may remain compressed if growth stays below 5%.

---

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

PG presents a **tension between deteriorating price momentum and improving fundamental valuation**. The stock has de-rated to the bottom of its annual P/E range (22.53 vs. 21.7–28.4) while delivering **+21.1%** YoY EPS growth and expanding net margins. A reversion to the midpoint P/E of ~25.5, combined with current earnings power, implies **~13% upside** to ~$16
PG.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Procter & Gamble (PG)**
**Decision Date: 2026-01-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 10-K, 2025-08-04)**
Procter & Gamble is a world-leading multinational consumer goods company with a diversified portfolio of daily-use products sold in approximately 180 countries. Its business model is built on sustainable value creation via balanced top- and bottom-line growth, driven by product superiority across five vectors (performance, packaging, communication, retail execution, value). The company invests in R&D and consumer insights, leverages multiple distribution channels (retail, e-commerce, DTC), and relies on continuous productivity improvements to fuel investments and shareholder returns. It holds leadership or significant market share in highly competitive markets that include both branded and private-label products. The business is subject to extensive global regulation (FDA, FTC, EPA, etc.), with compliance expenditures not expected to materially impact FY2026. Human capital (~109,000 employees as of 2025-06-30) and sustainability initiatives, including a 2040 net-zero ambition, are integral to strategy.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Capital Allocation & Investor Sentiment:** Noted for 69 consecutive years of dividend growth and recommended as a defensive, dividend-paying value stock (**2025-12-30**).
*   **Financial Guidance (from 2025-12-23):** Outlook includes all-in sales growth of 1%-5%, organic sales growth roughly flat to +4%, and core EPS of $6.83 to $7.09 (midpoint ~$6.96).
*   **Stock Performance & Sentiment:** Shares rallied to $145.13, described as being "back in the market’s good graces" after a choppy year for consumer staples (**2025-12-16**).
*   **Strategic Outlook (2025-12-19):** Assessment of 2025 "strategic recalibration" notes a forward-looking EPS growth estimate of 2.6% for 2026. Rationale cited is confidence in adaptability, with key risks including emerging market pressure and pace of innovation adoption.
*   **Growth Drivers & Catalysts:** Remote catalysts noted: productivity gains, cost management, tariffs, and share repurchases (**2025-11-30**). Targeted innovation (e.g., vertical video microsoap "The Golden Pear Affair" on **2025-12-11**) is ongoing but not seen as a near-term valuation superseding factor.
*   **Market Environment:** Projected growth in relevant categories like the electric toothbrush market (CAGR 7.90% to 2033, **2025-12-21**) and the global personal hygiene market (**2025-12-05**) may provide tailwinds.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Confirmed Risks:**
    *   **Regulatory/Legal:** P&G faces a lawsuit alleging lead contamination in Tampax tampons, claiming consumer misleading and safety risks (**2025-12-08**).
    *   **Competition:** Markets are highly competitive with pressure from both branded and private-label products (**10-K**).
    *   **Growth Profile:** Latest analyst view notes growth is driven by product mix and pricing, with volume stagnation, and a lack of immediate catalysts for significant upside (**2025-11-22**).
    *   **Operational/External:** Risks noted include pressure in emerging markets and the pace of innovation adoption (**2025-12-19**). Supply chain subject to price fluctuations and potential tariff impact (**10-K**). Full delivery on some sustainability ambitions is hindered by unavailable scalable solutions (**10-K**).
*   **Confirmed Offsets:**
    *   **Brand & Market Position:** Described as holding leadership or significant market share in key segments (**10-K**), with stable margins (**2025-11-22**).
    *   **Defensive Characteristics:** Consistently highlighted as a defensive, dividend-paying stock suitable for market downturns (**2025-12-30, 2025-12-17**).
    *   **Innovation & Marketing:** Ongoing investment in R&D and innovative marketing (e.g., **2025-12-11** collaboration) to drive brand loyalty.
    *   **Capital Return:** Guidance explicitly includes expectations for capital return alongside sales and EPS growth (**2025-12-23**).

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Positive:** The franchise is fundamentally strong, characterized by global scale, brand leadership, pricing power, and defensive earnings with a long dividend growth history. Guidance indicates stable, if moderate, organic growth prospects supported by innovation and cost management.
**Negative:** The near-term investment case is tempered by volume stagnation, reliance on pricing, specific legal risk (Tampax lawsuit), and muted growth catalysts. Competitive and macroeconomic (tariffs, emerging markets) pressures persist.
**Neutral:** The qualitative picture is balanced. The company's durability and dividend reliability are clear positives, but these are offset by constrained near-term growth dynamics and specific reputational risks, resulting in a neutral aggregate outlook.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'net margin expansion from 17.2% to 18.6% (supplied January-to-December figures)' but the raw evidence shows net_margin=0.172 on 2025-01-02 and net_margin=0.186 on 2025-12-31. The report's framing of 'January-to-December' is acceptable but the claim that 'the price decline has more than offset fundamental improvement' is a directional assertion not directly supported by the valuation data — the P/E compression could reflect multiple factors beyond earnings quality.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; the claim is directionally consistent with the data (P/E fell while EPS rose). Minor framing issue only.

corrections.issue: Quant report claims 'The entire earnings beat appears to come from below the operating line — likely favorable tax, interest, or non-operating items.' The raw evidence shows operating income grew YoY from 5.80B to 5.86B (+1.0%) while net income grew from 3.99B to 4.78B (+19.9%). The below-the-line contribution is real, but the report's characterization of operating margin as 'contracted slightly (26.7% → 26.2%)' uses prior Q1 figures (2024-09-30: 5.80/21.74=26.7%) which is correct. However, the report does not note that the YoY gross profit growth was only +1.6% vs +3.0% revenue growth, meaning cost of revenue also expanded — this is an additional nuance worth flagging.
corrections.required_action: No correction needed; the below-the-line earnings quality concern is valid and well-supported. The gross margin compression detail is already implied in the report's margin table.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'Shares rallied to $145.13, described as being back in the market's good graces' (2025-12-16). The raw price data shows the stock at $143.31 on 2025-12-31, meaning the 'rally' was subsequently reversed. The qual report does not flag this reversal, which could give an overly optimistic near-term sentiment read.
corrections.required_action: PM should note that the 2025-12-16 sentiment snapshot was transient; the stock subsequently declined ~1.3% to $143.31 by year-end, consistent with the quant report's negative momentum narrative.

unsupported_claims: : The qual report's characterization of shares being 'back in the market's good graces' (citing 2025-12-16 at $145.13) is not contradicted by the raw evidence but is incomplete — the stock closed at $143.31 on 2025-12-31, erasing the rally. This is an omission rather than an unsupported claim.

HON.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Assessment: Honeywell International Inc. (HON) as of 2026-01-01**

**1. Financial Trajectory**
- **Revenue & Earnings:** Quarterly revenue peaked at $9.73B (Q3 2024) and fell YoY by -2.7% in Q2 2025 to $9.32B. Despite revenue weakness, quarterly EPS grew QoQ from $2.22 to $2.45 (+10.4%) and YoY from $2.36 to $2.45 (+3.8%). TTM net income is $6.01B.
- **Margins:** Gross margin expanded from 34.3% (Q4 2023) to 39.3% (Q2 2025). Operating margin improved from 11.4% to 25.4% over the same period. TTM net margin is ~16.0% ($6.01B/$37.55B).
- **Cash Flow:** Not provided; trajectory unavailable.
- **Balance Sheet:** Total assets grew 27.5% from $61.52B (Q4 2023) to $78.42B (Q2 2025). Total equity increased only 1.3% (to $16.65B), while total debt surged 64.7% to $30.24B. Debt-to-equity (latest) is 1.541.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality:** Net income consistently trails operating income (e.g., Q2 2025: operating income $2.37B vs. net income $1.57B), indicating significant interest/tax burden. TTM operating income is $9.12B versus net income of $6.01B. Cash flow data is unavailable to assess conversion.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Leverage has deteriorated: debt grew from $18.36B to $30.24B while equity was stagnant ($16.43B to $16.65B). High asset-to-equity leverage (4.7x) and rising debt increase financial vulnerability.

**3. Valuation vs. Own History**
- Latest PE (22.395) is within its 2025 range (19.8–26.0), nearer the lower end. P/B (7.1) is also mid-range (6.3–8.2). Earnings yield is 4.5%. Current valuation is neither stretched nor deeply discounted relative to its recent past.

**4. Price Context vs. Fundamentals**
- Price (2025-12-31: $195.09) is down -9.8% annually and -1.5% quarterly, with 52-week range $169.05–$227.82. The annual price decline (-9.8%) exceeds the modest YoY EPS growth (+3.8%), reflecting PE contraction from ~24.4 (Jan 2025) to 22.4 (Dec 2025). Fundamental path shows revenue softening but margin expansion, while balance-sheet leverage increased.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside**
- **Upside:** Operating margin expansion to 25.4% (Q2 2025) versus 11.4% (Q4 2023) could drive sustainable earnings growth if revenue stabilizes. Valuation near range lows (~22x PE) offers room for multiple expansion on positive triggers.
- **Downside:** Revenue decline YoY (-2.7% in Q2 2025) may persist. Debt increase (64.7% over 6 quarters) elevates interest risk and balance-sheet fragility. Lack of cash-flow visibility raises concerns about earnings quality and dividend sustainability.

**6. Provisional Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The quantitative profile is cautious: although margin expansion supports earnings and valuation is not extreme, declining revenue and rapidly rising debt present material risks. The absence of cash-flow data further clouds quality assessment. Risk/reward appears neutral to slightly negative given the fundamental leverage build and top-line pressure.
HON.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Honeywell International (HON)**
**Decision Date:** 2026-01-01
**Evidence Window:** Point-in-time evidence ending before 2025-12-22.

### 1. Business/Franchise Read from Supplied Narrative
Based on the evidence, Honeywell's franchise is undergoing significant structural transformation. The company is in a "breakup phase" (2025-11-23, 2025-12-02) involving a planned portfolio reorganization that includes the spin-off of its Advanced Materials business (referred to as "Solstice spin") and a strategic acquisition. This positions the company as a diversified industrial conglomerate with active portfolio management aimed at unlocking value and focusing on key sectors like aerospace, automation, and energy transition (2025-11-28, 2025-12-02).

### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)
*   **Portfolio Actions:** The spin-off of its Advanced Materials business and the pending £1.8 billion acquisition of Johnson Matthey's Catalyst Technologies (expected H1 2026) are core corporate actions (2025-11-23, 2025-12-02).
*   **Guidance & Financials:** On 2025-12-22, Honeywell revised its Q4 2025 outlook and provided supplemental financials for the planned spin-off.
*   **Legal/Regulatory:** The company disclosed a $470 million legal charge in Q4 2025 related to a settlement of a lawsuit with Flexjet (2025-12-22). Separately, it faced an $81 million SEC action concerning past internal accounting controls (2025-12-11).
*   **Analyst Sentiment:** Bank of America Securities issued a double-downgrade to "Underperform" on 2025-11-18, citing challenges in the portfolio reorganization and a lack of near-term EPS growth catalysts for 2026.

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Execution Risk:** The planned breakup and spin-off are viewed as challenging, with specific criticism that the "Solstice spin and Aerospace CEO have not matched expectations," potentially leading to peer-lagging performance (2025-11-18).
    *   **Financial & Legal Overhang:** The significant $470 million Flexjet settlement charge and $81 million SEC penalty represent direct financial hits and signal potential weaknesses in contract management and internal controls (2025-12-22, 2025-12-11).
    *   **Near-Term Catalyst Deficit:** The downgrade highlighted a "lack of EPS growth in 2026," suggesting the current share price may not have immediate valuation support (2025-11-18).
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Strategic Positioning:** The Catalyst Technologies acquisition is intended to add capabilities in sustainable fuels and hydrogen, aligning with the long-term energy transition (2025-12-02).
    *   **Potential Unlocking of Value:** The broader "breakup phase" and spin-offs are viewed by some as a path to unlock upside and drive future growth (2025-11-23, 2025-12-15).
    *   **Macroeconomic Tailwinds:** Some analysis points to a shift in monetary policy (Fed pivot) as a potential short-term catalyst (2025-12-15).

### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Neutral.** The narrative is bifurcated. The confirmed evidence reveals material near-term pressures: a major downgrade, significant legal charges, and explicit concerns about execution and a near-term earnings catalyst. However, the strategic narrative of a corporate breakup aimed at long-term value creation and positioning in secular growth markets provides a countervailing optimistic thesis. The assessment is Neutral as the high-conviction negative short-term developments (legal costs, analyst skepticism) are balanced against the unproven long-term strategic rationale of the restructuring.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Gross margin expanded from 34.3% (Q4 2023) to 39.3% (Q2 2025)' and 'Operating margin improved from 11.4% to 25.4%.' The Q4 2023 gross margin from the raw data is 3.24B/9.44B = 34.3%, which checks. However, the Q4 2023 operating margin is 1.08B/9.44B = 11.4%, which also checks. The Q2 2025 gross margin is 3.66B/9.32B = 39.3% and operating margin is 2.37B/9.32B = 25.4%, which also checks. These are consistent. However, the report does not flag that the Q4 2023 operating margin of 11.4% appears to be an outlier (likely depressed by one-time items) compared to other quarters (Q1 2024: 22.2%, Q2 2024: 23.0%, Q3 2024: 23.6%, Q1 2025: 25.3%). The dramatic 'improvement' from 11.4% to 25.4% is partly a base-effect artifact rather than a clean margin expansion trend. The TTM operating margin from the raw data is 9.12B/37.55B = 24.3%, which is more representative.
corrections.required_action: Note that the Q4 2023 operating margin of 11.4% is an outlier quarter; the margin expansion narrative should be contextualized against the more representative ~22-25% range seen in other quarters, not just the depressed Q4 2023 base.

corrections.issue: The qual report references a '$470 million legal charge' and separately an '$81 million SEC action,' but the raw evidence from Honeywell's own investor page (2025-12-22) states the Flexjet charge is 'approximately $300 million, net of tax benefit of $70 million.' The $470M figure is the gross pre-tax charge. The report does not distinguish between gross and net-of-tax figures, which could overstate the earnings impact.
corrections.required_action: Clarify that the $470M is the gross pre-tax Flexjet charge; the net-of-tax impact is approximately $300M per Honeywell's supplemental disclosure.

corrections.issue: The qual report states 'Bank of America Securities issued a double-downgrade to Underperform on 2025-11-18' and characterizes this as a 'major downgrade.' The raw evidence confirms the downgrade from Buy to Underperform (a two-notch cut). However, the qual report also references a 'Fed pivot' as a potential short-term catalyst (2025-12-15) without noting that this is speculative macro commentary from a Substack blog, not from Honeywell management or a credentialed analyst. This is a minor sourcing quality issue.
corrections.required_action: Flag that the 'Fed pivot' catalyst is sourced from an independent blog (equityanalysiseng.substack.com) and should carry lower evidentiary weight than analyst or management commentary.

unsupported_claims: : The quant report's claim that 'P/B (7.1) is also mid-range (6.3–8.2)' is supported by the raw data (range 6.3–8.2). No unsupported claim here.

: The quant report states 'TTM net margin is ~16.0% ($6.01B/$37.55B).' The raw data shows net_margin=0.151 (15.1%) in the valuation ratios. 6.01/37.55 = 16.0%, but the reported net_margin in the valuation panel is 0.151. This is a minor rounding discrepancy (15.1% vs 16.0%) likely due to different calculation windows, but the quant report should note the valuation panel shows 15.1%.

: The qual report states Honeywell is in a 'breakup phase' involving 'spin-off of its Advanced Materials business (referred to as Solstice spin).' The raw evidence confirms this from multiple sources (2025-11-23, 2025-12-02). Supported.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Honeywell is in a transitional period marked by aggressive portfolio restructuring (Advanced Materials spin-off, Catalyst Technologies acquisition) that creates both opportunity and uncertainty. Margin expansion is real but partly an artifact of a depressed Q4 2023 base, revenue is declining, and leverage has surged materially. The stock is fairly valued near historical PE lows, but the absence of near-term EPS growth catalysts and significant legal/execution overhangs warrant a neutral stance.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Honeywell is in a transitional period marked by aggressive portfolio restructuring (Advanced Materials spin-off, Catalyst Technologies acquisition) that creates both opportunity and uncertainty. Margin expansion is real but partly an artifact of a depressed Q4 2023 base, revenue is declining, and leverage has surged materially. The stock is fairly valued near historical PE lows, but the absence of near-term EPS growth catalysts and significant legal/execution overhangs warrant a neutral stance.
key_factors: Operating margin expanded to ~25% in recent quarters, but the quant report's comparison to Q4 2023's 11.4% overstates the trend—Q4 2023 was an outlier; the TTM operating margin is a more representative 24.3%, and other quarters in 2024 ranged 22-24% (verification note C1).

Debt surged 64.7% from $18.36B to $30.24B over six quarters while equity was essentially flat ($16.43B to $16.65B), pushing debt-to-equity to 1.54x and total assets-to-equity to 4.7x—material balance-sheet fragility.
Revenue declined YoY by -2.7% in Q2 2025 to $9.32B, and Bank of America's double-downgrade to Underperform (2025-11-18) explicitly cited a lack of near-term EPS growth catalysts for 2026.

The $470M gross pre-tax Flexjet settlement charge (net ~$300M after tax, per 2025-12-22 disclosure) and $81M SEC penalty (2025-12-11) represent direct financial hits and signal governance/contract management weaknesses, though the net-of-tax impact is less severe than the headline figure (verification note C2).

nd fulfillment services, health and wellness, and financial services to provide our customers a broader set of offerings to meet expanding needs.
Seasonal Aspects of Operations.
Our business is seasonal to a certain extent and varies by country due to different national and religious holidays, festivals and customs, as well as different weather patterns. Historically, our highest sales volume for each segment has occurred in the fourth quarter of our fiscal year.
Suppliers and Supply Chain.
As a retailer and warehouse club operator, we utilize a global supply chain that includes both U.S. and international suppliers from whom we purchase the merchandise that we sell in our stores, clubs and online. In many instances, we purchase merchandise from producers located near the stores and clubs in which such merchandise will be sold, particularly products in the "fresh" category. Consistent with applicable laws, we offer our suppliers the opportunity to efficiently sell significant quantities of their products to us. These relationships enable us to obtain pricing that reflects the volume, certainty and cost-effectiveness these arrangements provide to such suppliers, which in turn enables us to provide low prices to our customers. Our suppliers are subject to standards of conduct, including requirements that they comply with local labor laws, local worker safety laws and other applicable laws. Our ability to acquire from our suppliers the assortment and volume of products we wish to offer to our customers, to receive those products within the required time through our supply chain and to distribute those products to our stores and clubs, determines, along with other supply chain logistics matters (such as containers or port access for example), in part, our in-stock levels in our stores and clubs and the attractiveness of our merchandise assortment we offer to our customers and members.
Intellectual Property.
We regard our trademarks, service marks, copyrights, patents, domain names, trade dress, trade secrets, proprietary technologies and similar intellectual property as important to our success, and with respect to our associates, customers and others, we rely on trademark, copyright, and patent laws, trade-secret protection, and confidentiality and/or license agreements to protect our proprietary rights. We have registered, or applied for the registration of, a number of U.S. and international domain names, trademarks, service marks and copyrights. Additionally, we have filed U.S. and international patent applications covering certain of our proprietary technology. We have licensed in the past, and expect that we may license in the future, certain of our proprietary rights to third parties.
Government Regulation.
As a company with global operations, we are subject to the laws of the United States and multiple foreign jurisdictions in which we operate and the rules and regulations of various governing bodies, which may differ among jurisdictions. For additional information, see the risk factors herein in "Item 1A. Risk Factors" under the sub-caption "Legal, Tax, Regulatory, Compliance, Reputational and Other Risks."
10
Our Shared Value Priorities
We seek to operate our business in a way that creates shared value: We believe we maximize long-term value and create competitive advantage for the Company by delivering for our stakeholders, including our customers, associates, shareholders, suppliers, business partners and communities. We believe that addressing their needs builds the value of our business, including by enhancing trust, creating new business opportunities, managing cost and risk, building capabilities for future advantage and strengthening the underlying systems on which we rely.
We prioritize issues that offer the greatest potential for Walmart to create long-term shared value: Issues that rank high in relevance to our business, importance to building and maintaining stakeholder trust and in which Walmart has the ability to be an effective actor. Our current priorities are categorized into four broad themes: opportunity, sustainability, community and ethics and integrity.
- Opportunity. We believe that promoting economic opportunity helps Walmart attract and retain the talent we need to run our business, respond to customer needs, and promote economic resilience in the places where we operate. Our approach includes creating opportunities for Walmart associates (as further described in the Human Capital Management section below), our suppliers, workers in supply chains and the communities we serve. We offer good entry-level jobs in addition to multiple career pathways; endeavor to cultivate a culture of belonging—rooted in respect for the individual—where associates and customers feel seen, supported and connected; and provide tens of thousands of local, national and global suppliers the opportunity to grow with us.
- Sustainability. Walmart's sustainability efforts endeavor to create and preserve long-term business value through increasing the efficiency and resilience of our operations, promoting surety of supply, sourcing responsibly and enhancing our product assortment. With respect to people, we aim to respect the dignity of people throughout our supply chain, create economic opportunity for people working in supply chains, and sell safer, healthier products. With respect to the environment, our efforts aim to enhance the sustainability of product supply chains; preserve, protect, and restore nature; reduce waste; procure renewable sources of energy; and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
- Community. We believe that Walmart thrives in strong, resilient communities, and we endeavor to help communities thrive. We aim to strengthen communities by providing convenient access to affordable, quality goods and services through our omni-channel retail model and everyday low prices; contributing to the economic vitality of communities by providing quality jobs, training and career paths; investing in local suppliers; and supporting organizations and causes that matter to our customers and associates.
- Ethics and Integrity. We believe that people want to work for and do business with a company they can trust, and Walmart works to create a culture that inspires trust with our stakeholders. We seek to achieve this, by focusing on promoting ethics and compliance, governance and oversight, responsibly engaging in public policy activities, using data and technology responsibly and respecting human rights.
We periodically publish information on our shared value priorities, strategies, progress, and challenges on the environmental, social and governance ("ESG") reporting section of our corporate website and may update those disclosures from time to time. Nothing on our website, including our ESG reporting, documents or sections thereof, shall be deemed incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K or incorporated by reference into any of our other filings with the SEC.
Human Capital Management
Our associates – powered by technology – play a critical role in delivering on our purpose to help people save money and live better. Our business is focused on serving people and this is delivered by our approximately 2.1 million associates around the world with approximately 1.6 million associates in the U.S. and approximately 0.5 million associates internationally. In the U.S., approximately 92% of our associates are hourly and approximately 68% of our associates are full-time.
We believe our people make the difference, and we are focused on investing in the growth and well-being of our associates, investing in digital experiences to improve their quality of work and creating a culture of belonging. An important part of our focus is to provide opportunities for associates to grow and learn. WMT.quantitative_report:
**WMT Quantitative Assessment**  
*Decision date: 2026-01-01; All data point-in-time prior*

---

### 1. Trajectory of Revenue, Earnings, Margins, Cash-Flow, and Balance Sheet  
- **Revenue**: Q3 2025 (Oct 31) revenue = 179.50B, +5.8% YoY from 169.59B in Q3 2024. TTM revenue = 703.06B.  
- **Earnings**: Q3 2025 EPS diluted = 0.77, +35.1% YoY from 0.57 in Q3 2024, but -12.5% QoQ from 0.88 in Q2 2025. TTM net income = 23.30B.  
- **Margins** (Q3 2025): Gross margin = 24.9% (gross profit 44.79B / revenue 179.50B). Operating margin = approximately 3.7% (operating income 6.70B / revenue 179.50B); supplied operating_margin = 0.043. Net margin = 0.028.  
- **Cash-Flow**: Unavailable for all periods (explicitly marked as n/a).  
- **Balance Sheet**: Total equity increased to 102.21B (Q3 2025) from 97.42B (Q4 2024) and 90.35B (Q4 2023). Total debt decreased to 36.00B (Q4 2024) from 39.58B (Q4 2023). Total assets = 288.65B (Q3 2025). Inconsistent liability data for Q2 and Q1 2025 (reported as negative).

### 2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience  
- **Earnings Quality**: YoY EPS growth of +35.1% and net income growth of +29.1% indicate positive momentum, but QoQ EPS decline of -12.5% and net income decline of -14.9% show volatility. TTM net margin of 0.028 is below the 0.036 level seen in early 2025. Cash flow data unavailable, limiting quality assessment.  
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Equity growing (from 90.35B to 102.21B), debt declining (from 39.58B to 36.00B by Q4 2024). Equity-to-assets ratio improved, but inconsistent liability reporting for intermediate quarters introduces ambiguity.

### 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History  
- **Latest Valuation (2025-12-31)**: PE diluted = 46.024, PB = 9.929, PS = 1.309, earnings yield = 0.022.  
- **Historical Context**: Over the 2025 window, PE ranged from 28.8 to 48.2, PB from 6.8 to 10.4. In sparse history, PE increased from 30.624 (Jan 2025) to 46.024 (Dec 2025), PB from 7.715 to 9.929, while net margin fell from 0.036 to 0.028 and ROE declined from 0.252 to 0.216. Current PE and PB are near the upper end of their ranges.

### 4. Price Level and Return Context Versus Fundamental Path  
- **Price**: Latest close = 111.41 (2025-12-31), annual return = +21.5% (from 91.66 on 2024-12-27), quarterly return = +9.3% (from 101.96 on 2025-10-01).  
- **Range**: 52-week high = 117.45, low = 79.81.  
- **Fundamental Alignment**: Price appreciation (+21.5% annual) elevated relative to revenue growth (+5.8% YoY) and amid margin contraction (net margin fell from 0.036 to 0.028). Valuation expansion (PE up from ~30 to 46) outpaces earnings quality improvement.

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside  
- **Upside**: Strong YoY revenue growth (+5.8%) and EPS growth (+35.1% YoY). Balance sheet shows equity expansion and debt reduction. TTM operating income = 28.98B supports core profitability.  
- **Downside**: High valuation multiples (PE 46.024, PB 9.929) near historical peaks. Declining profitability ratios (net margin 0.028, ROE 0.216) and quarterly earnings volatility. Cash flow unsupplied, obscuring free cash flow sustainability. Potential price correction risk if growth moderates.

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion  
Based on supplied numbers, risk/reward is skewed adversely: valuation has expanded significantly (PE +50% from Jan 2025) despite declining net margin and ROE, while revenue growth is moderate. The lack of cash flow data and balance sheet inconsistencies further increase uncertainty. Quantitative indicators suggest limited upside buffer at current levels, with downside risk from valuation reversion or further margin erosion.
WMT.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Walmart Inc. (WMT)**
**Decision Date: 2026-01-01**
**Evidence Cutoff: 2025-12-31**

### 1. Business/Franchise Read from Supplied Narrative
Based on the 10-K filing dated 2025-03-14, Walmart is a global, technology-powered omni-channel retailer serving approximately 270 million customers weekly across more than 10,750 stores and eCommerce websites in 19 countries. Its operations are divided into three segments: Walmart U.S. (69% of net sales in fiscal 2025), Walmart International (18%), and Sam's Club U.S. (13%). The company's strategy is anchored in its "Everyday Low Price" (EDLP) philosophy and "Everyday Low Cost" (EDLC) operational discipline, with a stated goal of becoming a more digital, customer-centric ecosystem. Key strategic pillars include enhancing omni-channel integration (e.g., Walmart+, pickup/delivery), growing higher-margin ancillary businesses (advertising, marketplace, fulfillment, financial services), and leveraging its scale for price leadership. The franchise benefits from massive global scale, a vast physical footprint, and a supply chain that includes 164 U.S. and 184 international distribution facilities.

### 2. Dated Recent Developments (Point-in-Time)
**Guidance & Performance:**
- **2025-11-20:** Walmart raised its full-year sales and earnings outlook after reporting fiscal Q3 results that beat expectations, driven by strong e-commerce growth and new customer acquisition across income levels (sources: Fox Business, CNBC, massmarketretailers.com).
- **2025-12-19:** Walmart raised its full-year guidance again, citing strong execution heading into the holiday season (source: ts2.tech).

**Corporate Actions & Partnerships:**
- **2025-12-22:** Walmart was noted to have nearly doubled its stock price since a February 2024 stock split and to have recently partnered with OpenAI (source: news summary).
- **2025-12-28:** Symbotic, an AI-powered robotic warehouse automation company, was highlighted as having Walmart as a major client (source: news summary).

**Industry & Consumer Trends:**
- **2025-12-23:** U.S. holiday spending was projected to surpass $1 trillion for the first time in 2025, with growth largely driven by inflation and tariffs rather than volume increases, indicating a "K-shaped" consumer environment (source: news summary).
- **2025-12-24:** The FDA approved the first oral GLP-1 weight-loss medication (Wegovy pill), leading major food companies to adjust menus toward higher-protein, smaller portions—a trend relevant to Walmart's grocery business (source: news summary).

**Regulatory & Legal:**
- **2025-12-15:** Walmart and other major retailers objected to a proposed settlement involving Visa and Mastercard (source: Reuters via web context).
- **2025-12-16:** Walmart sought to exit a class action related to card practices (source: paymentsdive.com).
- **2025-12-17:** PepsiCo and Walmart faced a federal lawsuit alleging a long-running price-fixing scheme (source: pymnts.com).
- **2025-12-23:** Short-term risk was noted from regulatory/legal headlines around a recalled infant formula and potential expanded litigation (source: ts2.tech).
- **2025-12-05:** A news item discussed potential negligence claims and lawsuits arising from a fire at a Walmart store (source: blog.founders.illinois.edu).

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
**Risks:**
1. **Elevated Legal & Regulatory Exposure:** The company is involved in multiple lawsuits and regulatory disputes (price-fixing, card practices, product liability, fire negligence). These pose financial liability, reputational risk, and potential operational distractions. (Evidence from 2025-12-05 to 2025-12-23)
2. **Consumer Spending & Competitive Pressure:** Holiday growth was attributed to inflation and tariffs, with a bifurcated consumer; Walmart's core value-seeking customer base may be pressured. The retail environment is described as "highly competitive" with new business models and well-funded entrants intensifying pressure. (10-K competition section; 2025-12-23 holiday spending article)
3. **Shifting Consumer Preferences:** The rapid adoption of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs could alter grocery purchasing habits, requiring swift adaptation of product assortment and merchandising. (2025-12-24 article)
4. **International Complexity:** The International segment involves managing diverse regulatory environments, currency fluctuations, and the need to continuously reshape the portfolio for profitable growth, as evidenced by recent exits from certain African markets. (10-K business section)

**Offsets:**
1. **Demonstrated Execution & Momentum:** The company has consistently beaten expectations and raised guidance, indicating strong operational control and relevance to consumers. (2025-11-20 and 2025-12-19 guidance updates)
2. **Omni-Channel & Technology Investments:** Significant investments in e-commerce, supply chain automation (e.g., partnership with Symbotic), and AI (partnership with OpenAI) are enhancing convenience and efficiency, forming a competitive moat. (10-K; 2025-12-28 article)
3. **Strategic Pricing Power & Scale:** The foundational EDLP/EDLC model, backed by global scale, allows Walmart to maintain price leadership and customer trust, which is particularly valuable in inflationary or uncertain economic times. (10-K strategy section)
4. **Diversified Growth Engines:** The expansion of higher-margin businesses like advertising, marketplace services, and financial services (e.g., PhonePe) diversifies revenue streams and leverages existing customer traffic. (10-K business description)

### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Positive.** The confirmed evidence points to strong recent operational execution, successful guidance raises, and strategic investments that bolster the franchise's long-term competitive position in omni-channel retail. While material legal/regulatory risks are present and the consumer spending environment is nuanced, these are partially offset by Walmart's scale, pricing power, and demonstrated ability to adapt and grow. The overall narrative suggests a durable business with positive momentum.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'operating margin = approximately 3.7% (operating income 6.70B / revenue 179.50B)' but then notes 'supplied operating_margin = 0.043'. The raw evidence confirms operating_margin=0.043 (4.3%). The 3.7% figure is arithmetically correct from the Q3 income statement (6.70/179.50=3.73%), but the report presents both without reconciling the discrepancy. The 0.043 likely reflects a TTM or different basis calculation. This creates confusion about which margin is authoritative.
corrections.required_action: Clarify that the 3.7% is the Q3 quarterly operating margin while 0.043 is the supplied trailing measure, and note the basis difference explicitly.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'net margin fell from 0.036 to 0.028' comparing Jan 2025 to Dec 2025. The raw evidence confirms this trajectory in the sparse history. However, the report also says 'TTM net margin of 0.028 is below the 0.036 level seen in early 2025' — the 0.036 in early 2025 was a point-in-time ratio snapshot, not necessarily a TTM figure. The comparison may conflate different measurement bases.
corrections.required_action: Distinguish between point-in-time net_margin snapshots and TTM net margin when comparing across periods.

corrections.issue: Qual report references 'Walmart raised its full-year guidance again' on 2025-12-19 citing ts2.tech. The raw qualitative evidence confirms this item. However, the qual report also states guidance was raised 'citing strong execution heading into the holiday season' while the raw evidence says 'raised full-year guidance again, citing strong execution heading into the holidays. Reuters reported Walmart's U.S. comparable sales growth' — the qual report omits the Reuters-reported comparable sales growth detail, which is a material operational metric.
corrections.required_action: Consider noting the comparable sales growth detail from the 2025-12-19 source for completeness.

unsupported_claims: Quant report claims 'Inconsistent liability data for Q2 and Q1 2025 (reported as negative)' — the raw evidence does show total_liabilities=-96.55B (Q2 2025) and -90.34B (Q1 2025), which is indeed anomalous. However, the report does not flag that this likely reflects a data artifact (negative liabilities would imply negative equity-plus-assets relationship) rather than genuinely negative liabilities. The report should note this is likely a data quality issue rather than a real balance sheet feature.
Quant report states 'TTM operating income = 28.98B supports core profitability' — the raw evidence confirms TTM operating_income=28.98B, so this is supported. No issue.

Qual report states 'recently partnered with OpenAI (source: news summary)' — the raw evidence from 2025-12-22 confirms 'Walmart has nearly doubled its stock price since a February 2024 stock split and to have recently partnered with OpenAI'. Supported.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Walmart is a high-quality, operationally strong retailer with demonstrated execution momentum and strategic investments in omni-channel and AI, but the stock's valuation has expanded aggressively (PE ~46, near historical highs) despite declining net margins and ROE, leaving limited risk-adjusted upside at current levels. The quantitative risk/reward is roughly balanced—strong franchise quality offsets stretched multiples, but a meaningful re-rating catalyst is absent.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Walmart is a high-quality, operationally strong retailer with demonstrated execution momentum and strategic investments in omni-channel and AI, but the stock's valuation has expanded aggressively (PE ~46, near historical highs) despite declining net margins and ROE, leaving limited risk-adjusted upside at current levels. The quantitative risk/reward is roughly balanced—strong franchise quality offsets stretched multiples, but a meaningful re-rating catalyst is absent.
key_factors: Valuation stretched: PE of 46.0 (Dec 2025) is near the top of its 2025 range (28.8–48.2) and up ~50% from Jan 2025, while net margin declined from 0.036 to 0.028 and ROE fell from 0.252 to 0.216 over the same period, indicating price appreciation has outpaced fundamental improvement.

Strong operational execution: Walmart raised full-year sales and earnings guidance twice (2025-11-20 and 2025-12-19), with Q3 2025 revenue of 179.50B (+5.8% YoY) and EPS of 0.77 (+35.1% YoY), reflecting robust e-commerce growth and customer acquisition across income levels.

Balance sheet resilience: Total equity grew to 102.21B (Q3 2025) from 90.35B (Q4 2023) while total debt declined to 36.00B (Q4 2024) from 39.58B (Q4 2023), providing financial flexibility; TTM operating income of 28.98B confirms core profitability.

Legal and regulatory headwinds: Multiple active lawsuits including a federal price-fixing suit with PepsiCo (2025-12-17), card-practices class action (2025-12-16), and product liability matters create tail risk and potential financial/reputational distraction.
